judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports hit all five expectations with essentially equivalent substance and citations; Report 2 is marginally more thorough overall, additionally flagging a mitigation-description mismatch (rollback vs. reroute) and a process-gap around the ADR review requirement, though these are outside the scored expectations.

report_1.evidence: The timeline shows failures began at 14:03 and the FIRST ALERT did not fire until 14:50 — a 47-minute detection gap, with on-call acknowledging at 15:05. "Within minutes" and "immediately" misrepresent a ~47-minute detection latency and ~62-minute response. (Draft lines 6–8 vs. `larkpay-comms/incidents/OPS-412-timeline.md`.)

report_2.evidence: `incidents/OPS-412-timeline.md`: failures began 14:03; the first alert fired at 14:50 — a 47-minute detection gap, not minutes.

report_1.evidence: `incidents/OPS-412-timeline.md` states: "1,284 transactions failed and required manual reprocessing; 37 were unrecoverable and refunded." Customers demonstrably lost transactions.

report_2.evidence: `incidents/OPS-412-timeline.md` states 1,284 transactions failed and required manual reprocessing, and 37 were unrecoverable and refunded. Customers were directly and materially affected.

report_1.evidence: April uptime was 99.77% (`metrics/uptime-2026.csv`), below the contractual 99.9% monthly SLA. Per `docs/sla-commitments.md`: "Any calendar month below 99.9% is a breach and owes service credits per the customer agreement (credits scale with the depth of the breach)."

report_2.evidence: `metrics/uptime-2026.csv` shows April 2026 at 99.77%, and `docs/sla-commitments.md` says any month below 99.9% is a breach that owes credits scaling with breach depth. Affected customers are owed credits; a postmortem to them cannot silently omit this.

report_1.evidence: It assigns blame to an individual — violates style-guide rule 1. "The outage was caused by the on-call engineer deploying an untested config change" (draft lines 12–13) directly breaks `docs/customer-comms-style-guide.md` rule 1: "Never assign blame to an individual."

report_2.evidence: Naming "the on-call engineer" violates the style guide (individual blame + role name). `docs/customer-comms-style-guide.md` rule 1 (never assign blame to an individual) and rule 2 (never expose internal role names). Line 12 does both. Reframe as a process/systemic cause.

report_1.evidence: It leads with a reliability brag, not customer impact — violates style-guide rule 3. The draft opens with the uptime claim (line 3) and only vaguely mentions "some checkout requests failed." Rule 3 requires leading with customer impact and remediation. The document should open with what happened to customers (failed/refunded transactions, duration ~14:03–15:41) and what LarkPay is doing about it.

report_2.evidence: The draft leads with a reliability brag, not with customer impact/remediation. `docs/customer-comms-style-guide.md` rule 3 requires leading with customer impact and remediation; the draft opens (lines 3, 16–19) with uptime and culture claims. Restructure to lead with what happened to customers and what is being done.
